evaluation.manager_summary: Strong empathy-driven call with a caller in early relapse. Patricia validated the caller's experience, identified aftercare as a gap, collected insurance information, and set a same-day callback. Two compliance concerns: implicit outcome promises through 'designing treatment differently' language and closing reassurance that implies success rather than process.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent immediately acknowledged Alexis's disclosure with validation ('Six months of sobriety is real — that's not nothing'), normalized relapse without minimizing it, and used her name throughout. The tone was warm, non-judgmental, and deeply human—never transactional.

Agent collected carrier name, member ID, and group number efficiently. She framed verification as part of moving forward ('I'm going to verify your benefits and flag the aftercare concern') and did not use jargon. Missing only explicit verification hedge language.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Consider adding a quick hedge like 'pending verification' when discussing benefits to stay compliant.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent gathered treatment history and identified aftercare gap, which are relevant contextual details. However, no substance use specifics were captured (frequency, last use, co-occurring conditions), and no severity indicators were explored beyond the relapse disclosure.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Follow up on substance type, recent use patterns, and any withdrawal concerns to give clinical team better intake data.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent correctly identified this as a non-crisis relapse situation requiring timely but not same-day response. The afternoon callback (before 3pm) is appropriate for recent relapse without withdrawal or safety signals. No urgency manufacturing detected.
Caller is the patient herself, confirmed by self-disclosure ('I've been sober for six months').

Agent acknowledged the 'rehab didn't work' objection and explored what was missing ('What did the last program not give you that you think you needed?'). However, the phrase 'the question isn't whether treatment can work for you — it's whether this time we design it differently' creates an implicit success expectation that crosses into soft outcome territory, even though contextualized by aftercare planning.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Avoid framing that implies designing treatment differently will ensure success; focus on process differences without outcome implication.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent set a specific callback window ('this afternoon', 'before three'), clarified who would call ('your intake coordinator'), stated what they would cover ('how our continuing care program works'), and confirmed caller availability ('after two').

Two compliance concerns: (1) 'So the question isn't whether treatment can work for you — it's whether this time we design it differently' implies that redesigning treatment will produce success, creating an outcome expectation; (2) 'We'll make sure the plan is [different]' at closing is a soft outcome promise. Both instances frame success as solvable through design, which crosses into predictive territory.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Avoid language that implies treatment success through design differences; focus on process ('we build aftercare into the program') without outcome implication ('this time will be different').

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Thank you for telling me that, Alexis. Six months of sobriety is real — that's not nothing. Relapse doesn't erase that.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Can I ask: when you say rehab 'didn't work,' what does that mean to you? What were you hoping would happen that didn't?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Alexis — coming back after a relapse is one of the hardest things to do. The fact that you made this call tells me something important about you.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Yes. Summit Select. Member ID BP63244887, group GRP-514222.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Thank you. I'm going to verify your benefits and flag the aftercare concern for your intake coordinator.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I thought I'd be fixed. I went in, did the 30 days, came out, and eventually ended up back where I started.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: What did the last program not give you that you think you needed?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Aftercare. I left and there was nothing. No follow-up, no support group, nothing.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Can they reach you this afternoon?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: You'll hear from us before three.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: That's a really honest answer. The thing I want to offer you — not as reassurance, but as a different frame — is that 30-day residential works for some people and doesn't for others

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: So the question isn't whether treatment can work for you — it's whether this time we design it differently.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: That's one of the most common reasons people relapse — and it's something we specifically address. Our aftercare planning starts on day one, not day thirty.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'm going to verify your benefits and flag the aftercare concern for your intake coordinator. They'll walk you through how our continuing care program works.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: You'll hear from us before three.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: So the question isn't whether treatment can work for you — it's whether this time we design it differently.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: We'll make sure the plan is.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: There's research showing that people who relapse after treatment and re-enter treatment have better long-term outcomes than those who never return.
